topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the cramped bar hung thick with cheap beer and the cloying scent of desperation. I nursed my whiskey, my gaze fixed on the stage where some upstart was butchering my melody. Every strained note, every clumsy transition, felt like a personal affront. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping near my temple. I shifted in my seat, the scrape of my chair against the worn floorboards sounding unnaturally loud in the sudden lull between songs. When the final, grating chord faded, I didn't applaud. I just slowly drained the last of my drink, the burn in my throat mirroring the sourness in my chest.

[story 2]

It happened during a crowded festival set. I was watching from the periphery, leaning against a concrete barrier, trying to look absorbed in my phone. Then, the familiar, haunting progression of my signature piece started up, played by a stranger. The sound was technically proficient, yes, but utterly hollow. I felt a cold tightening in my stomach, a sensation like ice spreading through my veins. I straightened up abruptly, my posture stiffening. I watched the performer with narrowed eyes, cataloging every misplaced gesture, every overblown flourish. When they finished, I didn't move to approach. I simply turned away, the noise of the crowd suddenly muted, leaving only the sharp, unpleasant echo of that inferior performance ringing in my ears.

[story 3]

I was backstage, waiting for my turn, when the sound bled through the thin door. It was my song, stripped bare, yet somehow… corrupted. A band I vaguely recognized was playing it, and their interpretation was clumsy, a cheap imitation draped over genuine emotion. I stood motionless, my hands clenched into fists inside my coat pockets. My breathing hitched, shallow and rapid. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of intense irritation. I took a slow, deliberate breath, focusing on the texture of the dusty floor beneath my boots, refusing to let my attention drift toward the source of the unwelcome noise. When the set concluded, I didn't acknowledge the applause; I just walked toward the exit, needing distance from the sound.
